speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I give you a little chair, a little table there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, do we have one?

speaker: interviewee
text: There.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll get it. Don't worry, you sit.

speaker: interviewee
text: There.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a mess.

speaker: interviewee
text: The little one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pick one out of the mess.

speaker: interviewer
text: No one will see it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera is rolling and frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: Surviving the Shoah, interviewer Penelope Toltz, T-O-L-T-Z. I am interviewing Martha Golan, G-O-L-A-N. We are doing this on the 20th of April, 1995 in English at number 2A [INAUDIBLE] 4 Buckhurst Avenue, Point Piper, Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling and frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: Surviving the Shoah project, interviewer Penelope Toltz. I'm interviewing Martha Golan on the 20th of April, 1995 in English and number 2A/4 Buckhurst Avenue, Point Piper, Sydney, Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cameras rolling and frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Golan, your name is Martha Golan. Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-R-T-H-A, Martha. Golan is G-O-L-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your maiden name Mrs. Golan?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Preisich, P-R-E-I-S-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on 4th of October, 1918 in Levice. Spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-V-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: It had two names. It had a Hungarian name as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Levice is that Czech name in Czechoslovakia. Then when we became Hungarians, it has a Hungarian name, which is Leva, L-E-V-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was Leva a small town or a large town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a town of about 13,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- and what did you parents do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father had a farm outside of Leva about 20-- 20 minutes by car.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your father's name please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Victor Preisich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: V-I-C-T-O-R. Preisich like before-- P-R-E-I-S-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my name-- mother's name was Margaret Pick,

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she spell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her born name, Pick-- Margaret Pick-- P-I-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Golan, when you were living in Leva, you were living there from the time you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born there, yes. And I lived there until-- until we left Leva for-- to go to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were little girl, what sort of education did you have? Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well first, I went to primary school in-- in Levice, and then to high school, which was a gymnasium for eight years. That was in Levice. Then I made my leaving or my matriculation they call it. Then I went to university to study medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whereabouts was university?

speaker: interviewee
text: The university in Prague, the Charles University in Prague. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you finish medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't finish medicine. I just was there until '39 when the university was closed. And then I went home back to Levice. I had only four semesters. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I lived in Levice. And in '39, we became Hungary. I wanted to continue my studies in Budapest, but I couldn't. There was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was numerus clausus.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not permitted?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't go to university.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of farm did your father run?

speaker: interviewee
text: With animals, cows, horses and wheat, sugar-- sugar--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sugar--

speaker: interviewee
text: --beets, tobacco, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a big farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was big. It was big, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he run it himself?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went every morning by car and came back in late in the afternoon. And during, only during the harvest time, we lived out there for two months in the school holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in an apartment or a house in Levice?

speaker: interviewee
text: A house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, when you were young before the war, was there any anti-Semitism in Levice?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't feel it. I didn't feel it. But it was a very friendly town and everybody knew everybody. And I couldn't feel anti-Semitism. It was different when we became Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What percentage of the town was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were-- I said 13,000 inhabitants. About 1,500 Jewish people, 500 Jewish families say, in Levice. All my family, all my grandparents, uncles, aunts, cousins, everybody was there in Levice.

speaker: interviewer
text: A large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Large family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brothers and sisters of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your parents who lived there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brothers, yes. Because I never had brothers or sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was an only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were an only child. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You went right through school. And you didn't notice any Semitism, but did you-- were you a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in that sense that we had a kosher household. And we celebrated all the high holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a synagogue in Levice?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one-- on synagogue, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of synagogue was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the ladies were upstairs and the men downstairs when you called it orthodox, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you remember the name of your rabbi, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Rabbi Lieberman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had family?

speaker: interviewee
text: An old gentleman.

speaker: interviewer
text: An old gentleman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very old.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a family, with grown up sons, lawyers, doctors, his sons. He was old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what sort of-- when you went to study in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Prague, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Prague, did you find that there was a difference when you went to Prague, that there was a different atmosphere, or that you had any anti-Semitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague, no. On the university, not at all, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was not. Was there a Jewish quota for the university in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in Czechoslovakia. There was no quota or nothing. There was-- religion wasn't even mentioned on the University. But when we became Hungarian, then there was numerus nullus.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said you became Hungarian, do you remember what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That part, that was in 1939 when that part. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Golan, tell me a little bit about life in Prague as a university student?

speaker: interviewee
text: What shall I tell you? Well, that was the first time I was away from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did you have friends they from Levice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from Levice. We all lived in the same so-called boarding house. I had many friends, and we got monthly parcels or weekly parcels from home. Everybody invited everybody and ate the goodies the home. And during normal days, we studied.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to keep kosher, or did that not come up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. Kosher was for my father's sake.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was more religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, what happened in 1939? How did you know something was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in '39 that part of Czechoslovakia where I lived was attached to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very close to the Hungarian border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not only the town. The whole district was attached to Hungary. And it was a big slice of Slovakia which became Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what began to change then? What happened? How did it-- how did it show itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then came-- then slowly the Jewish laws came in, then were restrictions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You couldn't-- well, the-- the shops were taken-- taken away from Jewish people. The whole structure of life changed and we kept more within the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed more at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: More at home, but life was normal. Life was absolutely normal until the German came in in '44. In March '44 when the German came in, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: It all changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: It all changed and we started to wear the yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. We'll get on to that in a moment, Mrs. Golan.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sorry, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I wanted to know was when you came home, did you just stay at home, or did you go and continue studying something else or somewhere else, after you'd left Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after I left Prague, and we-- yes. Then I-- in Budapest, I did a nursing course--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not allowed to continue medicine, but you were allowed to do nursing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, in a-- in the Jewish hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And you graduated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you specialize in anything in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less in babies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Young babies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Babies, yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, life went on. But then was it in 1944 the Germans came, and then what happened to your part of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then-- then SS and the Gendarmerie, we were frightened. We were not allowed to go out in the evening. It was a curfew. Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to wear the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to wear the star. That was the first restriction. We had to hand in our cars, our bicycles, our jewelry. Everything was restricted.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you still lived in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still lived in our home until-- until when they came in in March. And we lived in our home until end of April. Then the-- we went to a so-called ghetto, which was certain streets in Levice were said that these are the ghetto streets. And we could go and live there. So we lived in one of the friend's houses, I don't know, a few five or six families together. And from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it barred, or was there fences or guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, nothing. Only, it was known that that is the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people leave the ghetto daily? Did they go to work? Did your father still go to the farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: People just stayed. How did people pass the time? What did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen to the wireless, to the news.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father's farm? Did someone else keep running it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. I don't know. I never got it back, and I never asked for it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: When it came to June of that year--

speaker: interviewee
text: When it came?

speaker: interviewer
text: June of that year--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Levice then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the last three weeks, we lived in that ghetto. Then from there on the 13th of June, we were-- everybody, not only from Levice, the Jewish people, but from all over the vicinity from the little villages-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] we went to the railway station with little parcels, whatever we were allowed to take. I think 50 kilos or something like that. And we were put in cattle wagons and off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- they were [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: --a large number of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1,500 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1,500 people. And from that, only hundred came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did it take you to get?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took-- it took us three days, three horrible days. Because the cattle wagon was-- was sealed from the outside. There was no window, no door, nothing, nothing. We were there about-- it wasn't hardly place to sit. Because if we wanted to sit, somebody has to stand. And we were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] changing. I was sitting for half and hour, somebody else was. It was crammed. And-- and it was inhuman. It was one bucket in the corner for our bodily functions in front of everybody. I mean, it was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed two leave the truck at all in  the three days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, only when we arrived somewhere in three-- three day's time. We didn't know if it's day or night, because it was dark there all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. We didn't know. We didn't even know that we left Hungary, only when we arrived and the door was-- the wagon was unsealed. And then we had arrived. And there we saw men in striped uniforms and striped caps. And they were jumping and pulling us down and all what we had our belongings. And they said, give the little children to the grandmothers. We didn't know what that-- what does that mean. The men were taken away straight away. So my father, we were worried about my father, because he had angina. And we were worried if he has his pills with him or it was in my mother's handbag. Anyhow, then I stayed with by mother and aunties and cousins. And the women went one-by-one. There was a very good-looking German officer standing. That was Mengele. And he was deciding who goes left and who goes right. Left was straight to the-- of course, we didn't know all that. Only the elderly and the children went to the left. And the younger ones went to the right. When I came with my mother in front of Mengele, he showed my mother left and to me right. And I said [SPEAKING GERMAN] That is in German-- that is my mother. And he said [SPEAKING GERMAN]. She will follow in a car. So we were happy that she will come by car. She doesn't have to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever see her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, never saw anyone-- anyone who went to the left again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course, he went to the left. I didn't see that. But, of course, he went to the left. He was 61, and he was-- he wasn't very healthy. He had heart problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about what happened day to day to the people that went to the right, the people the did stay alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- well-- when we-- there we walked to a big room where we had to undress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then showers started-- a cold shower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were really even happy, we were refreshed. For three days, we didn't wash and didn't do-- so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after the shower, then we were sent to the next room. There we were shaven. Our hair was shaven off, also under the arm, under-- the pubic hair, everything. From there, we went to the next room. There, we got our clothing. Everybody got something. No underwear, only one-- one something-- regardless how big you are, how small you are, what size you are. I got a dark brown see-through summer frock without underwear. It was long to my ankle. And when my friend, my best friend saw me, she said, god, Martha. You are ugly, I mean, [LAUGHS] without hair in that outfit. So then we walked again, and walked, and walked until we came on-- in-- in. Well, when we arrived at the gate, there was a music, an orchestra-- a woman's orchestra in striped-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they were the [NON-ENGLISH], the prisoner's orchestra. And there was a big gate. And there was written arbeit macht frei. Work makes you free. So we thought we will work, we will do something. But in all, anyhow, that wasn't like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived, finally, to a big barrack which was all empty, no beds, no bunks, no straw, nothing. And it was for about 500 or 800 people. We were pushed in. It was, again, so crammed that we couldn't-- couldn't-- not everybody could stretch out lying down. Anyhow, that's where we spent about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] five, six weeks doing absolutely, absolutely nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the barracks, we were only at night til the morning. During the day, it wasn't allowed to enter that barrack. And there, I've never seen-- we couldn't work it out where we are. There was not one tree, not a little grass-- nothing, only mud and dirt and dry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- we really couldn't work it out where we are. But then they told us, the old ones, that we are in Poland and that is Auschwitz and that they are crematoria. And they-- they really said to us the first night, you see that fire there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are your parents are burning there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's where the older prisoners who were jealous on the Hungarian ones, that we had it good until then. We were still at home, and they were suffering already for years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, did you just stand around outside during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were just sitting around. And very interesting, we were cooking all the time. We were cooking and say recipes and what will we eat the first time when we come home and just--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what food did they actually give you?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you can call that food-- in the morning, we got such a brown liquid, which was supposed to be coffee or something, which in the beginning, we used for washing our hands and washing our face. But later on, we were drinking it. And a little piece of bread-- bread, which was suppose to last you the whole day. Some people were cutting it to slices, and that's for lunch, that's for evening. I, for myself, the minute I got it, I ate it. I [LAUGHS] didn't waste-- not a morsel. I ate everything straight away. And the-- in the evening, we got such a grey-ish-- I don't know what it was, barley or whatever it was. But we made a point of it, that we are eating it just to keep up the strengths. And to-- the-- the biggest problem was thirst. There was no water at all-- not for washing, not for drinking. The thirst was worse than hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because it was summer, too, wasn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was summer. It was June, June, July. Well, my-- my friends and my family were always thirsty. I was lucky. I don't know the feeling of thirst. So I-- I was only hungry, not thirsty.

speaker: interviewer
text: After five or six weeks, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: After five or six weeks--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then one morning, we noticed that I have spots on my tummy. And, well, we knew straight away that this infectious disease, scarlet fever. Because that was raging then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't know. Shall we say something, or shall we keep it quiet? But then we thought-- I was always together with my cousin and with my two aunties. We said, well, I can infect everybody. We have to say it. So we went to the-- the barrack had a doctor. We showed her. And she says yes, that's [NON-ENGLISH]. Like, next morning, you are coming with me. We are going to the truck. So next morning, on the corner every morning, there was a truck collecting dead infectious diseases or the dead people. So we were marching to that truck with the doctor and with my cousin. And the doctor said, give her your shoes. You don't need them anymore. So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what she said. But Judzi-- is my cousins name-- she didn't want my shoes. So I stayed in my shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came to that corner. There is no truck. Where is the truck? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: That morning, a barrack was opened up for infectious diseases. And everybody who had scarlet fever or typhus came into that room. And I spent the next six weeks on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So although it was for infectious diseases, it wasn't a hospital or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a hospital. It was just a special barrack for infectious disease. Whoever survived it, survived it or not. I think during the whole six weeks, if I got half an aspirin, then it's-- I didn't get anything. But I was young, and I was not only six weeks away from home, from normal life, so I survived. I survived it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many people in the sick barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many, many. And in those six weeks, I made friends there. And in the meantime on the beginning, my family came to the window. They knocked on the window, and they could look in. But then they didn't come anymore. And then I heard that they were taken away from Auschwitz to work. So I stayed there, in there six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: After six weeks, I was supposed to be released into-- back into the lager. And I said, what shall I do now? Nobody there, no friend, no relative. Here, I know the people, and I am a nurse. So I said, I would like to stay there. Were-- were there any doctors or any medical people in the barrack at all apart from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all. No, I wasn't a medical. I was just a nurse. But they were girls who looked after us, lying on the straw and bringing us. It was actually much better than in the lager, because we had our straw bed. And we got our food there. And we don't have to stand every morning and every evening for that so-called [NON-ENGLISH], which was counting the heads for hours and hours in cold and in sun and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the same sort of food? Or was condition-- apart from the beds, were conditions any better, or was the food the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same, the same. By that time, we were not so hungry anymore. The stomach somehow gets used to-- shrinks and gets used to it. And the-- the beginning was the worst with the hunger. The beginning was very bad with the hunger. But then, you-- you got used to the situation. And, of course, you were loosing. I was loosing weight all the time. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: After the six weeks, you said you wanted to stay on and help in that hospital area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so I stayed there. And I was helping, too, with whatever there was to do to distribute the meal or to help whatever there was needed. And that was about for two, three weeks. Then Mengele-- that Dr. Mengele-- came in there. And he said, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. That means qualified nurses, step out. I stepped out. I didn't know it's good or bad. I stepped out. And that was the miracle, what really happened to me. Because I was taken from-- from there, from Auschwitz. I was first sent for-- for disinfection. I was dressed up very nicely. I got a black winter coat with fur Persian collar. Only one sleeve was different. I made. And from there, I went with one of the Aryans-- that is the whoever looked after Gestapo, the German woman-- to Hamburg, Germany in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Continue?

speaker: interviewer
text: I beg your pardon yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with her from-- directly from Birkenau-Auschwitz to Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel?

speaker: interviewee
text: On train on [NON-ENGLISH], normally like everybody else. And we arrived to Hamburg to a small camp with about 200 people. There was one doctor there and one nurse. And they needed another nurse, and that was me.
